title: Sanitation checkpoint: handwashing supplies
summary: Practical guidance for “Sanitation checkpoint: handwashing supplies” within “Water · Sanitation · Hygiene”. It supports safe collection, separation, treatment limits, storage, and conservation of water, produces a visible result, and states when to stop or move to a safer fallback.
content:
Practical guidance for “Sanitation checkpoint: handwashing supplies” within “Water · Sanitation · Hygiene”. It supports safe collection, separation, treatment limits, storage, and conservation of water, produces a visible result, and states when to stop or move to a safer fallback.

Start with: Use a written trigger, one accountable owner, a visible result, and a scheduled review for this exact task. This is the opening check for “Sanitation checkpoint: handwashing supplies” in “Water · Sanitation · Hygiene”.

Stop condition: Never assume clear-looking water is safe, and never mix unknown treatment chemicals. Apply this boundary to “Sanitation checkpoint: handwashing supplies” in “Water · Sanitation · Hygiene”.

Reduced fallback: If “Sanitation checkpoint: handwashing supplies” cannot be completed, do only the first verified step, mark the remaining capability unavailable, and use a protected supply confirmed safe by the competent authority.
materials: food-grade water containers — for “Sanitation checkpoint: handwashing supplies” in “Water · Sanitation · Hygiene”
clean caps or lids — for “Sanitation checkpoint: handwashing supplies” in “Water · Sanitation · Hygiene”
labels and marker — for “Sanitation checkpoint: handwashing supplies” in “Water · Sanitation · Hygiene”
separate collection and treated-water tools — for “Sanitation checkpoint: handwashing supplies” in “Water · Sanitation · Hygiene”
written local advisory — for “Sanitation checkpoint: handwashing supplies” in “Water · Sanitation · Hygiene”
steps: Use a written trigger, one accountable owner, a visible result, and a scheduled review for this exact task. This is the opening check for “Sanitation checkpoint: handwashing supplies” in “Water · Sanitation · Hygiene”.

Identify the source, likely contamination, intended use, and current official advisory. Record the result under “Water · Sanitation · Hygiene” before continuing “Sanitation checkpoint: handwashing supplies”.

Keep raw, treated, and wastewater containers physically separate and clearly labelled. Recheck this point before closing “Sanitation checkpoint: handwashing supplies” for “Water · Sanitation · Hygiene”.

Use only a treatment method appropriate to the suspected hazard; ordinary treatment does not remove every chemical or radioactive contaminant. Treat any uncertainty here as an unresolved item in “Sanitation checkpoint: handwashing supplies” for “Water · Sanitation · Hygiene”.
Protect the treated supply from hands, cups, animals, sunlight, leaks, and dirty caps. A second person should be able to verify this part of “Sanitation checkpoint: handwashing supplies”.

Log the fill date, treatment, users, remaining volume, and next rotation or inspection. Include the outcome of “Sanitation checkpoint: handwashing supplies” in the handover for “Water · Sanitation · Hygiene”.
warnings: Never assume clear-looking water is safe, and never mix unknown treatment chemicals. Apply this boundary to “Sanitation checkpoint: handwashing supplies” in “Water · Sanitation · Hygiene”.
Do not use containers that previously held fuel, pesticides, solvents, or other toxic products. Apply this boundary to “Sanitation checkpoint: handwashing supplies” in “Water · Sanitation · Hygiene”.
common_mistakes: Skipping the opening control makes “Sanitation checkpoint: handwashing supplies” unreliable: Use a written trigger, one accountable owner, a visible result, and a scheduled review for this exact task. Apply this boundary to “Sanitation checkpoint: handwashing supplies” in “Water · Sanitation · Hygiene”.

Treating the result as safe while ignoring this limit is a serious error: Never assume clear-looking water is safe, and never mix unknown treatment chemicals. Apply this boundary to “Sanitation checkpoint: handwashing supplies” in “Water · Sanitation · Hygiene”.

Failing to document the final handover action leaves the next person without a trustworthy starting point: Log the fill date, treatment, users, remaining volume, and next rotation or inspection. Apply this boundary to “Sanitation checkpoint: handwashing supplies” in “Water · Sanitation · Hygiene”.
alternatives: If “Sanitation checkpoint: handwashing supplies” cannot be completed, do only the first verified step, mark the remaining capability unavailable, and use a protected supply confirmed safe by the competent authority.
Use a dated paper note and spoken read-back for “Sanitation checkpoint: handwashing supplies” in “Water · Sanitation · Hygiene” when the normal form, device, or communication channel is unavailable.
failure_signs: Stop and reassess “Sanitation checkpoint: handwashing supplies” when treated water develops an unusual smell, colour, taste, sediment, damaged seal, or uncertain history. Apply this boundary to “Sanitation checkpoint: handwashing supplies” in “Water · Sanitation · Hygiene”.

Stop and reassess “Sanitation checkpoint: handwashing supplies” when illness appears among users or the source becomes affected by floodwater, sewage, chemicals, or an official warning. Apply this boundary to “Sanitation checkpoint: handwashing supplies” in “Water · Sanitation · Hygiene”.
when_not_to_use: Do not continue “Sanitation checkpoint: handwashing supplies” while the source may contain sewage, fuel, pesticides, salt, floodwater, radioactive material, or another hazard that the chosen treatment cannot remove. Apply this boundary to “Sanitation checkpoint: handwashing supplies” in “Water · Sanitation · Hygiene”.

This card is not authorization to declare unknown water safe, mix treatment chemicals, or use a container that previously held toxic products. Topic-specific stop check for “Sanitation checkpoint: handwashing supplies”: also follow this warning: “Never assume clear-looking water is safe, and never mix unknown treatment chemicals in “Sanitation checkpoint: handwashing supplies”.”. Apply this boundary to “Sanitation checkpoint: handwashing supplies” in “Water · Sanitation · Hygiene”.
short_term: Complete one verified cycle for “Sanitation checkpoint: handwashing supplies” in “Water · Sanitation · Hygiene”, record its owner, time, result, unresolved risk, and next review.
long_term: Review “Sanitation checkpoint: handwashing supplies” after real use in “Water · Sanitation · Hygiene”; replace failed materials, practise the handover, and update thresholds when official guidance or local conditions change.
if_method_fails: If the method described in “Sanitation checkpoint: handwashing supplies” gives no verifiable result, stop relying on it, isolate the uncertain output, record where it failed, and switch to a protected supply confirmed safe by the competent authority.
environment_notes: Adapt the guidance in “Sanitation checkpoint: handwashing supplies” for “Water · Sanitation · Hygiene” to weather, lighting, crowding, accessibility, power, water, transport, and available skill without removing the stop condition or independent check.

Practical guidance for “Sanitation checkpoint: pest and odour escalation” within “Water · Sanitation · Hygiene”. It supports safe collection, separation, treatment limits, storage, and conservation of water, produces a visible result, and states when to stop or move to a safer fallback.

Start with: Use a written trigger, one accountable owner, a visible result, and a scheduled review for this exact task. This is the opening check for “Sanitation checkpoint: pest and odour escalation” in “Water · Sanitation · Hygiene”.

Stop condition: Never assume clear-looking water is safe, and never mix unknown treatment chemicals. Apply this boundary to “Sanitation checkpoint: pest and odour escalation” in “Water · Sanitation · Hygiene”.

Reduced fallback: If “Sanitation checkpoint: pest and odour escalation” cannot be completed, do only the first verified step, mark the remaining capability unavailable, and use a protected supply confirmed safe by the competent authority.
materials: food-grade water containers — for “Sanitation checkpoint: pest and odour escalation” in “Water · Sanitation · Hygiene”
clean caps or lids — for “Sanitation checkpoint: pest and odour escalation” in “Water · Sanitation · Hygiene”
labels and marker — for “Sanitation checkpoint: pest and odour escalation” in “Water · Sanitation · Hygiene”
separate collection and treated-water tools — for “Sanitation checkpoint: pest and odour escalation” in “Water · Sanitation · Hygiene”
written local advisory — for “Sanitation checkpoint: pest and odour escalation” in “Water · Sanitation · Hygiene”
steps: Use a written trigger, one accountable owner, a visible result, and a scheduled review for this exact task. This is the opening check for “Sanitation checkpoint: pest and odour escalation” in “Water · Sanitation · Hygiene”.

Identify the source, likely contamination, intended use, and current official advisory. Record the result under “Water · Sanitation · Hygiene” before continuing “Sanitation checkpoint: pest and odour escalation”.

Keep raw, treated, and wastewater containers physically separate and clearly labelled. Recheck this point before closing “Sanitation checkpoint: pest and odour escalation” for “Water · Sanitation · Hygiene”.

Use only a treatment method appropriate to the suspected hazard; ordinary treatment does not remove every chemical or radioactive contaminant. Treat any uncertainty here as an unresolved item in “Sanitation checkpoint: pest and odour escalation” for “Water · Sanitation · Hygiene”.
Protect the treated supply from hands, cups, animals, sunlight, leaks, and dirty caps. A second person should be able to verify this part of “Sanitation checkpoint: pest and odour escalation”.

Log the fill date, treatment, users, remaining volume, and next rotation or inspection. Include the outcome of “Sanitation checkpoint: pest and odour escalation” in the handover for “Water · Sanitation · Hygiene”.
warnings: Never assume clear-looking water is safe, and never mix unknown treatment chemicals. Apply this boundary to “Sanitation checkpoint: pest and odour escalation” in “Water · Sanitation · Hygiene”.

Do not use containers that previously held fuel, pesticides, solvents, or other toxic products. Apply this boundary to “Sanitation checkpoint: pest and odour escalation” in “Water · Sanitation · Hygiene”.
common_mistakes: Skipping the opening control makes “Sanitation checkpoint: pest and odour escalation” unreliable: Use a written trigger, one accountable owner, a visible result, and a scheduled review for this exact task. Apply this boundary to “Sanitation checkpoint: pest and odour escalation” in “Water · Sanitation · Hygiene”.

Treating the result as safe while ignoring this limit is a serious error: Never assume clear-looking water is safe, and never mix unknown treatment chemicals. Apply this boundary to “Sanitation checkpoint: pest and odour escalation” in “Water · Sanitation · Hygiene”.

Failing to document the final handover action leaves the next person without a trustworthy starting point: Log the fill date, treatment, users, remaining volume, and next rotation or inspection. Apply this boundary to “Sanitation checkpoint: pest and odour escalation” in “Water · Sanitation · Hygiene”.
alternatives: If “Sanitation checkpoint: pest and odour escalation” cannot be completed, do only the first verified step, mark the remaining capability unavailable, and use a protected supply confirmed safe by the competent authority.

Use a dated paper note and spoken read-back for “Sanitation checkpoint: pest and odour escalation” in “Water · Sanitation · Hygiene” when the normal form, device, or communication channel is unavailable.
failure_signs: Stop and reassess “Sanitation checkpoint: pest and odour escalation” when treated water develops an unusual smell, colour, taste, sediment, damaged seal, or uncertain history. Apply this boundary to “Sanitation checkpoint: pest and odour escalation” in “Water · Sanitation · Hygiene”.

Stop and reassess “Sanitation checkpoint: pest and odour escalation” when illness appears among users or the source becomes affected by floodwater, sewage, chemicals, or an official warning. Apply this boundary to “Sanitation checkpoint: pest and odour escalation” in “Water · Sanitation · Hygiene”.
when_not_to_use: Do not continue “Sanitation checkpoint: pest and odour escalation” while the source may contain sewage, fuel, pesticides, salt, floodwater, radioactive material, or another hazard that the chosen treatment cannot remove. Apply this boundary to “Sanitation checkpoint: pest and odour escalation” in “Water · Sanitation · Hygiene”.

This card is not authorization to declare unknown water safe, mix treatment chemicals, or use a container that previously held toxic products. Topic-specific stop check for “Sanitation checkpoint: pest and odour escalation”: also follow this warning: “Never assume clear-looking water is safe, and never mix unknown treatment chemicals in “Sanitation checkpoint: pest and odour escalation”.”. Apply this boundary to “Sanitation checkpoint: pest and odour escalation” in “Water · Sanitation · Hygiene”.
short_term: Complete one verified cycle for “Sanitation checkpoint: pest and odour escalation” in “Water · Sanitation · Hygiene”, record its owner, time, result, unresolved risk, and next review.
long_term: Review “Sanitation checkpoint: pest and odour escalation” after real use in “Water · Sanitation · Hygiene”; replace failed materials, practise the handover, and update thresholds when official guidance or local conditions change.
if_method_fails: If the method described in “Sanitation checkpoint: pest and odour escalation” gives no verifiable result, stop relying on it, isolate the uncertain output, record where it failed, and switch to a protected supply confirmed safe by the competent authority.
environment_notes: Adapt the guidance in “Sanitation checkpoint: pest and odour escalation” for “Water · Sanitation · Hygiene” to weather, lighting, crowding, accessibility, power, water, transport, and available skill without removing the stop condition or independent check.
